name: Mechanics — Properties of Matter, Force, Motion, and Energy

sourceRefs.label: tnpscguru.in - Mechanics, Properties of Matter, Force, Motion & Energy Q&A
foundational definitions (Newton's three laws, KE/PE formulas) — standard physics, included for completeness even without a direct past-paper hit

researchNotes: OCR index search for 'lever', 'friction', 'momentum', 'kinetic', 'potential energy', 'simple machine' returned 2 hits, both false positives on unrelated content — physics formulas/diagrams don't OCR-search well by nature (numbers and symbols, not topic-naming prose). 5 solid questions sourced from a TNPSC prep aggregator cover real exam angles (motion-type identification, formula matching, circular motion, real-world physics applications); supplemented with standard textbook definitions of Newton's three laws and energy types, since these are foundational enough that the syllabus's own phrase ('properties of matter, force, motion, and energy') guarantees some direct-definition questions even without a specific sourced past-paper hit for each one.

reading.intro: This is the densest physics-fact clause in Unit I — TNPSC tests it through a mix of motion-type identification, formula-matching ('which of these equations is correct'), and real-world application questions (why X happens) rather than asking for bare definitions in isolation.

qa.question: If the distance travelled by a body is directly proportional to the square of time, what type of motion does it have?
qa.answer: Uniformly accelerated motion. (Distance ∝ t² is exactly the shape of the equation s = ½at² for motion starting from rest under constant acceleration.)

qa.question: Which of these basic mechanics equations are correctly stated? (i) Force = mass × velocity (ii) Acceleration = velocity/time (iii) Work = Force × displacement (iv) Power = Work/time
qa.answer: (ii), (iii) and (iv) are correct. (i) is wrong — Force = mass × ACCELERATION (F=ma), not mass × velocity (mass × velocity is actually the formula for momentum).

qa.question: In uniform circular motion, what is linear velocity always perpendicular to?
qa.answer: The radius vector (the line from the centre to the moving object) — this is why circular motion requires a constant centre-directed (centripetal) force even though speed stays constant.

qa.question: Why do rear-wheel-drive cars (engine in front) often need extra weight in the trunk, especially in one driving condition?
qa.answer: On icy roads — added weight over the rear (drive) wheels improves traction/grip, which is especially important on low-friction icy surfaces.

qa.question: Why is a water drop in space (near-zero gravity) spherical?
qa.answer: In the absence/near-absence of gravity, surface tension dominates, and a sphere is the shape with the least surface area for a given volume — so the drop naturally settles into a sphere to minimize surface energy.

qa.question: What are Newton's three laws of motion, in brief?
qa.answer: First law (inertia): a body stays at rest or in uniform motion unless acted on by a net external force. Second law: F = ma (force equals mass times acceleration). Third law: for every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction.

qa.question: What's the difference between kinetic energy and potential energy, with their formulas?
qa.answer: Kinetic energy (KE) is the energy of motion: KE = ½mv². Potential energy (PE) is stored energy due to position (commonly gravitational): PE = mgh (mass × gravity × height).

qa.question: What is momentum, and how is it different from force?
qa.answer: Momentum = mass × velocity (p = mv) — a property of a moving object. Force = mass × acceleration (F = ma) — what's needed to change that motion. A common wrong-answer trap is swapping these two formulas.

questions.prompt: A body's distance travelled is directly proportional to the square of elapsed time. This indicates:
questions.explanation: s ∝ t² matches s=½at², the signature of uniformly accelerated motion from rest.

questions.prompt: Which of the following equations is INCORRECT?
questions.explanation: Force = mass × acceleration, not mass × velocity (that's momentum).

questions.prompt: In uniform circular motion, the linear velocity at any instant is perpendicular to the:
questions.explanation: Perpendicular to the radius vector — this is the geometric basis of circular motion.

questions.prompt: Why is extra weight sometimes added to the trunk of a rear-wheel-drive car when driving on icy roads?
options: To improve traction on the drive wheels
questions.explanation: Extra weight over the rear (drive) wheels improves grip/traction on low-friction icy surfaces.

questions.prompt: A water droplet floating in zero gravity (e.g. in space) takes a spherical shape because a sphere:
options: Has maximum surface area for its volume
Has minimum surface area for its volume
Has maximum volume for its surface area
Is the easiest shape to photograph
questions.explanation: Minimum surface area for a given volume minimizes surface energy, which surface tension favors.

options: A body at rest stays at rest unless acted on by a force
For every action there is an equal and opposite reaction
Energy can neither be created nor destroyed

questions.prompt: The formula for kinetic energy of a moving body is:
The formula for gravitational potential energy is:
Momentum of a moving body is given by:
Which of Newton's laws is also known as the 'law of inertia'?
questions.explanation: The First Law (a body resists changes to its state of motion) is the law of inertia.
